title: Progress against dementia - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: Dementia prevalence has been falling sharply across developed countries for decades, with US rates in the 85-89 cohort dropping from three in ten forty years ago to one in ten by 2024, framed as a new instantiation of the Flynn Effect.

insights.claim: US dementia prevalence among 85-89 year-olds fell from three in ten forty years ago to just one in ten by 2024, per JAMA-published research by Stallard and colleagues.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A large secular decline in age-specific dementia reshapes long-term assumptions for elder-care demand, long-term-care insurance, and Alzheimer's-drug market sizing.
insights.quote: whereas 40 years ago three in every ten Americans aged 85-89 had dementia, by 2024 just one in ten had it
insights.locator: Mr Stallard has been working for a decade

insights.claim: Across six North American and European countries, the share of older people diagnosed with dementia fell 13% per decade between 1988 and 2015 in a study of nearly 50,000 people led by Frank Wolters of Erasmus Medical Centre.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: The decline is international and robust across datasets, strengthening the case that it is a real trend rather than a measurement artifact.
insights.locator: What is more, America is not the only beneficiary

insights.claim: Framingham Heart Study data show new dementia cases dropping ~20% per decade over ~40 years, and using narrow age bands Stallard calculates dementia rates declining 2.5-3% for each successive calendar-year cohort.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Cohort-level methodology confirms a steady generational improvement, useful for projecting future prevalence rather than extrapolating crude pooled rates.
insights.locator: Data from the Framingham Heart Study

insights.claim: Cowen frames the multi-decade decline in dementia as the new instantiation of the Flynn Effect, linking cognitive-aging improvement to the historical secular rise in measured intelligence.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Positions falling dementia as part of a broad, ongoing cognitive-improvement trend, a bullish lens on human-capital and healthspan trajectories.
insights.quote: You can think of this as the new instantiation of the Flynn Effect
insights.locator: Here is more from Jonathan Rosenthal
